metadata.summary: 7-phase systematic process for funnel improvement: map, measure, prioritize drops, diagnose, hypothesize, test, repeat. Rule: always fix the largest absolute user loss first.

content.title: Funnel Optimization Framework
content.summary: A 7-phase process for systematically improving conversion at each step of a user journey: map steps, measure rates, find the biggest drop, diagnose the cause, generate testable hypotheses, run A/B tests, and repeat on the next biggest drop. The rule: always fix the biggest absolute loss first, not the easiest step.

why.p: Every product funnel leaks. Without a structured process, teams guess which step to fix and waste effort on small gains. The framework ensures effort is directed at the highest-leverage drop-off and that changes are validated with data before being declared wins.

list.item: A product has measurable steps from entry to goal (signup, purchase, activation)
Overall conversion is below industry benchmarks or declining
You need to prioritize which step to optimize next
You have at least 7 days of tracking data per step
Fewer than 100 users per funnel step — sample size too small for meaningful diagnosis
No analytics instrumentation in place — map and measure steps first before optimizing
Product has not yet reached product-market fit — optimize retention before acquisition funnel

section.name: overview
section.p: The 7-phase process for systematically improving conversion: map, measure, find drop, diagnose, hypothesize, test, repeat.

rule.statement: Always fix the step with the largest absolute user loss first, not the step with the lowest percentage conversion.
rule.rationale: A step losing 6,500 users at 35% conversion outweighs a step losing 300 users at 9% conversion, even though the percentage looks worse on the smaller step.

section.name: phase-1-map-the-funnel
section.p: Define every step from first touch to goal. This foundation step is critical because ambiguous definitions lead to tracking errors that poison later diagnosis. Get explicit stakeholder agreement on what "conversion" means in your context before instrumenting a single event.

rule.statement: Define every step from first touch to goal, name each step with an event name, and get stakeholder agreement before measuring.
rule.rationale: Ambiguous step definitions cause tracking inconsistencies that make drop-off analysis unreliable. Rework a funnel definition mid-analysis is expensive.

example.note: SaaS signup funnel with concrete event names at each step: page_view_home, cta_clicked, signup_form_viewed, email_submitted, email_verified, profile_completed, first_action_completed. Each event is named, ordered, and tied to a real user action or system event.

section.name: phase-2-measure-each-step
section.p: Collect data across all steps for the minimum time window needed for valid analysis.

rule.statement: Collect minimum 30 days of data and segment by traffic source, device, and new vs. returning before drawing conclusions.
rule.rationale: Short windows miss weekly patterns; unsegmented data hides that mobile users drop at different steps than desktop users.

section.name: phase-3-find-the-biggest-drop
section.p: Identify which transition causes the largest absolute user loss.

rule.statement: Rank transitions by absolute users lost, not by drop percentage. Prioritize the transition with the highest user count lost.
rule.rationale: A 65% drop at step 1→2 losing 6,500 users is higher priority than a 30% drop at step 6→7 losing 461 users.

section.name: phase-4-diagnose-the-cause
section.p: Use multiple diagnostic methods to understand why users drop off: session recordings reveal user behavior and frustration points; heatmaps show which areas users click and scroll; exit surveys capture stated reasons (user-perceived); user interviews provide depth and uncover hidden friction. Diagnostics are inputs to hypothesis generation, not conclusions. Document all findings before moving to hypotheses.

list.item: Landing to CTA: unclear value prop, poor design, slow load, confusing positioning
CTA to form: too many fields, scary commitment language, unclear privacy terms
Form to submit: technical errors, confusing UX, field validation unclear, error messages unhelpful
Submit to verify: email in spam folder, long confirmation delay, unclear next step
Onboarding to activation: too complex, no guidance, value not shown, aha-moment missing

section.name: phase-5-generate-hypotheses
section.p: Create testable, data-grounded hypotheses for each diagnosed problem.

rule.statement: Write every hypothesis in IF/THEN/BECAUSE format: "If we [change X], then [metric] will improve by [Y%], because [diagnosis-based reason]."
rule.rationale: Vague hypotheses cannot be falsified; format enforces that each hypothesis is grounded in diagnosis, not guesswork.

section.name: phase-6-test-and-iterate
section.p: Run A/B tests prioritized by impact and confidence.

rule.statement: Prioritize hypotheses with ICE (Impact x Confidence x Ease). Test the highest ICE score first. Score 20-30 means test immediately.
rule.rationale: Without scoring, teams test what is easiest to build, not what will move the metric most.

section.name: phase-7-repeat
section.p: Iterate continuously on the next biggest drop-off.

rule.statement: After implementing a winner, move to the next biggest drop-off transition. Track cumulative conversion improvement over time.
rule.rationale: Funnel optimization is a continuous loop, not a one-time project.

section.name: common-funnel-types
section.p: Standard funnel shapes across product categories.

list.item: Marketing: Impression → Click → Landing Page → Signup → Activation → Purchase
SaaS: Visit → Signup → Onboarding → Activation → Engagement → Conversion → Retention
E-commerce: Visit → View Product → Add to Cart → Checkout Start → Payment → Confirmation
Mobile App: App Store → Download → Open → Onboarding → Core Action → Retention → Monetization

section.name: per-phase-checklist
section.p: Per-phase verification steps ensuring measurement quality, diagnosis depth, and test rigor before moving to the next phase.

list.item: Define all steps from entry to goal
Identify main funnel type (marketing, SaaS, e-commerce, mobile)
Name each step with an analytics event name
Identify entry point and conversion goal
Document alternate paths or user segments
Get stakeholder agreement on funnel definition
Set up event tracking for all steps
Collect minimum 30 days of historical data
Calculate conversion rate: (users at step N / users at step 1) x 100
Calculate drop rate: ((users at N - users at N+1) / users at N) x 100
Segment by: traffic source, device, new vs. returning
Verify data quality — no tracking errors, complete events
Rank each step transition by absolute users lost
Calculate percentage of total users lost per transition
Identify top 3 transitions by absolute loss
Confirm which drop has the largest financial impact
Review session recordings for the priority drop-off step
Check heatmaps (click and scroll behavior)
Run exit-intent survey at drop-off point
Conduct 5-10 user interviews for major product issues
Check technical error logs at that step
Compare mobile vs. desktop behavior
Measure page load speed at that step
Document all findings before generating hypotheses
Create 5-10 IF/THEN/BECAUSE statements
Each hypothesis grounded in Phase 4 diagnosis
Each hypothesis is testable (has a measurable metric)
Cover design, copy, and flow changes
Score each hypothesis with ICE (Impact, Confidence, Ease)
Rank hypotheses by ICE score; test highest first
Define control and variant clearly — change only one variable
Set test duration until statistical significance (p < 0.05)
Track primary metric plus secondary metrics
Monitor test daily for anomalies
Document winner and loser with statistical confidence
Implement winning test if lift exceeds threshold
Document learnings and surprising findings
Move to next hypothesis or next biggest drop
Set up weekly/monthly funnel review cadence
Track cumulative improvement over time

template.purpose: Funnel analysis document template for recording step metrics and hypotheses
LLM prompt for analyzing funnel drop-off and prioritizing fixes

section.name: limitations-and-gotchas
section.p: The framework assumes you can attribute users across steps (consistent IDs, deterministic events). Fragmented identity (cookieless, anon → identified) breaks the math; agents must validate ID stitching first.

"Biggest drop" prioritization underweights downstream value; a 50% drop at "viewed pricing" can be less critical than a 10% drop at "added card" if customer LTV concentrates at the bottom. Agents should also report "users-lost-weighted-by-LTV".
The ICE 1–10 scoring is subjective; without anchoring rubrics, two agents will produce different scores for the same hypothesis. Pin the rubric first.
The common-cause diagnostic table is a heuristic — real causes need session recordings plus interviews; agents must not finalize a diagnosis from analytics alone.

section.name: agentic-workflow
section.p: A funnel-framework agent runs as a 7-step pipeline aligned with the process: funnel-mapper writes the step + event spec, funnel-instrumenter adds events + verifies in dev, funnel-analyzer pulls 30-day data and produces the conversion table, leak-prioritizer ranks drops by absolute and LTV-weighted loss, diagnosis-runner orchestrates session-recording / heatmap / survey reads to compose root-cause notes, hypothesis-generator emits ICE-scored hypotheses using the IF/THEN/BECAUSE template, test-planner queues experiments.

Recommended subagents: funnel-mapper (input: product surface; output: ordered steps + event names matching the framework table format), funnel-analyzer (pulls cohort data from analytics emitting the conversion + drop-off table), leak-prioritizer (scores leaks by absolute users lost AND LTV-weighted lost revenue), diagnosis-runner (composes session-recording summaries + heatmap top-clicks + survey responses into a root-cause note), hypothesis-generator (emits 3–5 IF/THEN/BECAUSE hypotheses scored with ICE), test-planner (turns hypotheses into experiment specs with sample size + duration).
